title: I Saw Thee Under the Fig Tree?
summary: “Jesus answered and said unto him, Because I said unto thee, I saw thee under the fig tree, believest thou? thou shalt see greater things than these. And he saith unto him, Verily, verily, I say unto you, Hereafter ye shall see heaven open, and the angels of God ascending and descending upon the Son of man” John 1:50-51 The fig tree has two main connotations for Jews: it represents a reminder of the nation’s spiritual nakedness without salvation and, secondly, the promise of the coming Messiah who will usher in peace and salvation. Let us begin with the first one. “And the eyes of them both were opened, and they knew that they were naked; and they sewed fig leaves together, and made themselves aprons” (Genesis 3:7). We do not know the species of the tree of the knowledge of good and evil, but we know that the first humans sought to hide their shame with the leaves of the fig tree. Jews have a long tradition of praying under fig trees, and Nathaniel most probably was praying under a fig tree when Philip approached him. We are not told what he was praying for but Jesus said, “Before that Philip called thee, when thou wast under the fig tree, I saw thee” (John 1:48). Perhaps Nathanael prayed for the salvation of Israel and the world. It was no coincidence the Lord directed Philip to the fig tree. Saints of God in Europe, we must spend more time under the fig tree praying for the salvation of Europe. Jesus will see us – he will be moved by our tears and send a Philip to us.
